segments.text:
Chapter 3-Errors in Chemical Analysis
Errors Matter ( BALCO Scandal)

Errors Matter (Zika Epidemic)
Microcephaly
Abnormally small head associated with incomplete brain development
Can be caused by infections from the mosquito-borne virus Zika during a mother’s pregnancy
CDC recommends that pregnant women who travel/live in areas with known Zika outbreaks be tested for the virus
“Botched” Testing
Technicians at a Washington DC testing lab mistakenly diluted lab reagents, resulting in 409 Zika tests all coming back “negative.”
Retesting identified pregnant women who had the Zika virus.
Standard operating procedures (SOP) outline specific procedures to ensure the quality of the chemical analysis.

• Every measurement (physical and chemical) has some uncertainty, which is called experimental error .
•  Conclusions can be expressed with a high or a low degree of confidence but never with complete certainty.
• Individual results from a set of measurements are seldom the same hence consider the “best” estimate to be the central value for the set.

Types of Errors in Experimental Data
Systematic (or determinate) error
Random (or indeterminate) error
Gross error

Systematic (or determinate) error
• Causes the mean of a data set to differ from the accepted value. * Affect measurement accuracy .
• Causes all the results to be too high or too low .
• Systematic error can be discovered and corrected although it may not be easy.
• Represented with a constant bias in measurement results.
 Have a definite value and an assignable cause , same magnitude (reproducible) for measurements made in the same way.

Sources of Systematic Errors
• Instrumental errors - Caused by nonideal instrument behavior
Other examples….
• Method errors - Arise from nonideal chemical or physical behavior of analytical systems.
Examples….
• Personal errors - Result from carelessness, inattention or personal limitation of the experimenter.
Examples….

Random (or indeterminate) error
• Causes data to scatter more or less symmetrically around a mean value.
• Arises from the effects of uncontrolled (or uncontrollable) variables in the measurement.
• Affect measurement precision.
• Random error have an equal chance of being positive or negative.

• There is always some random error superimposed on the systematic error
• Always present and cannot be corrected but might be reduced by a better experiment.

Gross error
• Differ from indeterminate and determinate error.
• Occur occasionally and are often large and may cause a result to be high or low.
• Product of human errors.
• Lead to outliers , results that appear to differ significantly for all other data in a set of replicate measurements.

Detection of Systematic Method Errors
• Difficult to detect and correct
Analysis of standard samples
• Estimate bias by analyzing standard reference materials (SRMs), materials that contain one or more analytes at known concentration levels.
• SRMs can be purchased from government and industrial sources e.g., NIST.

Independent analysis
• A second  independent and reliable analytical 	method to measure the same quantity.
- The method should differ as much as 	possible from the one under study. Why?
Blank determination
• Blank → reagents and solvents used in a determination but no analyte.
•  Whenever feasible blanks may contain added constituents to simulate sample matrix.

• All the steps of the analysis are performed in a blank determination.
• Reveals errors due to interfering contaminants from the reagents and vessels used in the analysis.
Round robin experiment
• Different people in several laboratories analyze identical samples by the same or different methods.
•Disagreement beyond the estimate of random error is systematic error.

Eliminating Systematic Errors and Personal Errors
•  Regular maintenance of equipment…periodic calibration of equipment.
• Use standard addition or internal standard to correct for matrix effects.
Good laboratory practices…have a well-kept lab notebook to note unusual trends in experiments 	and to identify sources of errors.
•  Personal errors minimized by care and self-discipline.
 Check instrument readings, notebook 	entries and calculations systematically.

Reflection Moments
Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of random (or indeterminate) error?
A. It arises from uncontrolled variables in the measurement.
B. It cannot be eliminated completely.
C. It arises from a flaw in equipment or the design of an experiment.
D. It might be reduced by a better experiment.
E. It has an equal chance to be positive or negative.
2. A systematic error can be corrected by
A. increasing the number of measurements.
B. proper calibration.
C. always employing the same person to perform the analysis.
D. using a larger amount of sample.
E. statistical evaluation.
3. True or False: Random errors can be eliminated and reduced by better techniques.
4. True or False: Reproducible measurements are always close to the “true” value.

Accuracy and Precision
• Accuracy describes closeness of the measurement to the true or accepted value.
• Precision describes reproducibility of measurements.
* The closeness of results measured in exactly the same way.
*The precision of a measurement is readily determined by carefully replicated experiments.

Low accuracy, low precision
Low accuracy, high precision
High accuracy, low precision
High accuracy, high precision
Illustrating “ accuracy ” and “ precision ”
•  Results can be precise without being accurate, and
accurate without being precise.

Some analytical data illustrating “accuracy”
and “precision”
Benzyl isothiourea
hydrochloride
Nicotinic acid
Analyst 4: imprecise, inaccurate
Analyst 3: precise, inaccurate
Analyst 2: imprecise, accurate
Analyst 1: precise, accurate

Absolute and Relative Uncertainty
• Absolute uncertainty (absolute error) → the margin of uncertainty associated with a measurement.
• Relative uncertainty is a more useful quantity than the absolute uncertainty:

The Propagation of Uncertainity from Random Errors
• Most propagation of uncertainity computations deal with random error, not systematic error. The goal is always to eliminate systematic errors.
• The overall precision of an experimental result will depend on the random errors that occur in each individual step used to obtain this result.
• Error propagation is a method that can be used to help identify the major contributions to random errors in an analysis .

Addition and Subtraction
• For addition and subtraction, use absolute uncertainty.
Example

Multiplication and Division
• For multiplication and division convert all uncertainties into percent relative uncertainties.

Example
Mixed Operations

Question
A method for the detection of morphine is used to generate a calibration curve in which the assay response ( y ) is plotted versus morphine concentration ( x , in mg/L). This gives a straight line with a slope ( m ) of 0.253 and a y -intercept ( b ) of 0.010, where y = mx + b . The slope of this line has a standard deviation of ±0.009, and the standard deviation of the intercept is ±0.007. If the sample from an athlete gives a response of 0.541±0.015 in this method, what is the concentration of the morphine in the sample and estimated precision of this concentration?
Exponents and Logarithms
• Use relative uncertainity (e x /x), not percent relative uncertainity in calculations involving log x, ln x, 10 x and e x .

Question
One test performed with athlete urine samples is a measurement of the urine’s pH. This measurement ensures that such samples have not been altered to prevent detection of banned substances.
If a standard solution has a hydrogen ion concentration of 4.0 ( 0.2) x 10 -8 M, what will be the pH and the absolute uncertainty on this pH value?
If the pH of a urine sample is determined to be 6.00 (0.05), what will be the value and the absolute uncertainty of the hydrogen ion concentration in the sample

Assignment
3-4 a, c, 3-5 b, 3-7, 3,9
